title: Form submission model

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific HTML: Semantic and Accessible Web Documents draft for Form submission model; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A form groups controls for submission, with action, method, successful control names, and encoding determining the request.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A search form uses GET so its named query appears in a bookmarkable URL. Your goal is to build and explain a form's serialized submission without exposing sensitive values in a query URL. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Paragraph and inline meaning; Relative and absolute URLs. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: A form groups controls for submission, with action, method, successful control names, and encoding determining the request. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: GET and POST have different semantics, disabled controls are not successful, and client validation does not secure server processing. This draft therefore rejects: The form element automatically sends every visible control even when it lacks a name.

includes: Use form, action, method, names and values while distinguishing GET and POST semantics.
Build and explain a form's serialized submission without exposing sensitive values in a query URL.
excludes: GET and POST have different semantics, disabled controls are not successful, and client validation does not secure server processing.

outcomes: Build and explain a form's serialized submission without exposing sensitive values in a query URL.

examples.title: Form submission model: concrete case
examples.situation: A search form uses GET so its named query appears in a bookmarkable URL.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A search form uses GET so its named query appears in a bookmarkable URL.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Only successful named controls are submitted; choose method and action from request semantics and validate again on the server.
examples.result: The result follows because Only successful named controls are submitted; choose method and action from request semantics and validate again on the server.

examples.title: Form submission model: boundary case
examples.situation: GET and POST have different semantics, disabled controls are not successful, and client validation does not secure server processing.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Start from the documented boundary: GET and POST have different semantics, disabled controls are not successful, and client validation does not secure server processing.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: The form element automatically sends every visible control even when it lacks a name.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: Only successful named controls are submitted; choose method and action from request semantics and validate again on the server.

recap.summary: You should now be able to build and explain a form's serialized submission without exposing sensitive values in a query URL. Keep this boundary in view: GET and POST have different semantics, disabled controls are not successful, and client validation does not secure server processing.
